verses.text: Adam, Sheth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalaleel, Jered, Henoch, Methuselah, Lamech, Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Guidance: The Chronicler begins his entire work — which will climax in David, the temple, and the worship of the LORD — not with Israel, nor even with Abraham, but with one word: 'Adam.' Writing to the small, discouraged community that returned from Babylonian exile, the Chronicler reaches back to the very first man to anchor his readers' identity in the largest possible frame. Before Israel was a nation, before there was a covenant, there was the LORD's creation of humanity, and the line of promise runs unbroken from the first Adam down through the generations. The chapter is showing us, in this bare list from Adam to Noah's three sons, that the God of Israel is the God of all human history — not a tribal deity but the Creator from whom every person descends. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes span all of time: the same God who made Adam was at work through Seth, Enosh, and the long line down to Noah, and would carry that purpose forward to David and beyond. For a people who had lost their land, their temple, and their king, this genealogy was a declaration of belonging — they were not a footnote of history but heirs of a line that stretched back to creation itself and forward to God's unfinished promise. The chapter is teaching us that our identity is rooted not in our present circumstances but in the God who has been faithful across every generation since the beginning.

The sons of Japheth; Gomer, and Magog, and Madai, and Javan, and Tubal, and Meshech, and Tiras. And the sons of Gomer; Ashchenaz, and Riphath, and Togarmah. And the sons of Javan; Elishah, and Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim. Guidance: The genealogy now branches into the descendants of Noah's three sons, beginning with Japheth — the line from which the northern and western peoples (the seafaring and distant nations) descended. The Chronicler works from the outermost branches inward, naming the families of the nations before narrowing to the chosen line. The chapter is showing us that the table of nations (drawn from Genesis 10) belongs in Israel's story: the LORD is the God of Gomer and Magog and Javan and all the scattered peoples, not Israel alone. The chapter is teaching us the universal scope of God's concern. By recording the nations descended from Japheth, the Chronicler reminds the post-exilic community — surrounded by and intermingled with the nations — that all peoples share a common origin under the one Creator. The chapter is teaching us that the same God who would form Israel as His special people never ceased to be the God of all the families of the earth, a truth that points forward to the day when, through Abraham's seed, 'all the families of the earth' would be blessed, and people from every nation would be gathered into the worship of the LORD. The nations are named first and set in the frame; the chosen line will emerge from within this universal family, but never apart from it.

The sons of Ham; Cush, and Mizraim, Put, and Canaan. And the sons of Cush; Seba, and Havilah, and Sabta, and Raamah, and Sabtecha. And the sons of Raamah; Sheba, and Dedan. And Cush begat Nimrod: he began to be mighty upon the earth. And Mizraim begat Ludim, and Anamim, and Lehabim, and Naphtuhim, And Pathrusim, and Casluhim, (of whom came the Philistines,) and Caphthorim. And Canaan begat Zidon his firstborn, and Heth, The Jebusite also, and the Amorite, and the Girgashite, And the Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite, And the Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite. Guidance: The line of Ham produces the peoples who would loom large in Israel's later story — Cush (Ethiopia), Mizraim (Egypt, the land of bondage), and Canaan (whose descendants, the Jebusites, Amorites, Hivites and the rest, were the nations Israel would displace in the promised land). Two figures are singled out: Nimrod, who 'began to be mighty upon the earth' (the first empire-builder, founder of Babel and the eastern kingdoms), and the note that the Philistines came from Mizraim's line. The chapter is showing us that the very peoples who would become Israel's great adversaries — Egypt, the Canaanites, the Philistines, and Babylon's spiritual ancestor in Nimrod — are here set in their place within the one human family under God. The chapter is teaching us that even the nations who would oppose the LORD's people are not outside His sovereignty; He records their origins, He knows their generations, and He governs their rise and fall. The note about Nimrod — the first 'mighty one' and empire-builder — quietly reminds the post-exilic reader, fresh from Babylon, that the proud kingdoms of the earth, however mighty, are but branches on a family tree the LORD oversees. The chapter is teaching us that the God of Israel is sovereign over the mighty and the hostile nations alike; their power is real but derivative, and their place in history is held within His larger purpose, which runs not through the empire-builders but through the humble line of promise He is tracing toward David and, ultimately, the Messiah.

The sons of Shem; Elam, and Asshur, and Arphaxad, and Lud, and Aram, and Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Meshech. And Arphaxad begat Shelah, and Shelah begat Eber. And unto Eber were born two sons: the name of the one was Peleg; because in his days the earth was divided: and his brother's name was Joktan. And Joktan begat Almodad, and Sheleph, and Hazarmaveth, and Jerah, Hadoram also, and Uzal, and Diklah, And Ebal, and Abimael, and Sheba, And Ophir, and Havilah, and Jobab. All these were the sons of Joktan. Guidance: Now the genealogy turns to Shem — and here the line of promise runs, for it is through Shem that the chosen seed descends (Genesis had already pronounced 'Blessed be the LORD God of Shem,' Gen 9:26). The branch narrows toward Eber (from whom 'Hebrew' derives) and his son Peleg, in whose days 'the earth was divided' (the dispersion at Babel). The Chronicler still records the wider family — Joktan's many sons spreading into Arabia — but the eye of the genealogy is now fixed on the line that leads to Abraham. The chapter is showing us the principle of divine election operating through the generations: from all of humanity the LORD narrows His redemptive line to Shem, then to Eber, then to Peleg, drawing ever closer to the chosen family. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes in history are both universal and particular — He is the God of all the nations (Japheth, Ham, and the wider sons of Shem), yet He works His salvation through one chosen line. The chapter is teaching us that this narrowing is not favoritism but mission: the LORD chooses one family not to exclude the nations but to bless them through it, for the promise to Abraham (about to appear) was that in him all the families of the earth would be blessed. Shem, Arphaxad, Shelah, Eber, Peleg, Reu, Serug, Nahor, Terah, Abram; the same is Abraham. Guidance: The genealogy now races down the chosen line in a swift, deliberate cascade — Shem, Arphaxad, Shelah, Eber, Peleg, Reu, Serug, Nahor, Terah — to its goal: 'Abram; the same is Abraham.' The whole sweep from Shem to Abraham is compressed into a single breath, as if the Chronicler is hurrying to reach the man with whom the covenant of promise was made. The closing phrase, 'the same is Abraham,' marks the moment with quiet emphasis: this is the man whose name the LORD changed from Abram ('exalted father') to Abraham ('father of a multitude'), the recipient of the great promises of land, seed, and worldwide blessing. The chapter is showing us the LORD's purpose arriving at its covenantal anchor-point. Ten generations from Shem (as ten generations ran from Adam to Noah) bring us to Abraham, the father of the faithful. The chapter is teaching us that the long, patient unfolding of generations was always moving toward a goal — the LORD does not record names aimlessly but is tracing the thread of His promise to the one through whom redemption would come to all the world. The chapter is teaching the returned exiles that they are 'the children of Abraham,' heirs of the covenant promises that no exile could annul; the God who kept the line intact from Adam through Babel to Abraham, and from Abraham through every later trial, is the God who keeps His promises across all generations. The cascade of names is, at heart, a cascade of covenant faithfulness, arriving at the father of the people of God.

The sons of Abraham; Isaac, and Ishmael. These are their generations: The firstborn of Ishmael, Nebaioth; then Kedar, and Adbeel, and Mibsam, Mishma, and Dumah, Massa, Hadad, and Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. These are the sons of Ishmael. Guidance: Abraham's two sons are named — 'Isaac, and Ishmael' — and the Chronicler, following his pattern, deals first with the non-chosen line before returning to the line of promise. Ishmael's twelve sons (Nebaioth, Kedar, and the rest) are listed, the fathers of the Arabian tribes, fulfilling the LORD's promise to make Ishmael a great nation of twelve princes (Gen 17:20; 25:16). Having honored that promise in the record, the genealogy will set Ishmael aside to follow Isaac. The chapter is showing us, again, the gracious order of divine dealing: the LORD made real promises even to Ishmael, the son not chosen to carry the covenant, and those promises were kept — Ishmael became a great people. The chapter is teaching us that God's election of one line does not mean the abandonment of the others; He blessed Ishmael abundantly even as the covenant ran through Isaac. The chapter is teaching us the difference between the blessings of providence (given widely, even to the non-elect line) and the covenant of redemption (carried through the chosen seed). Ishmael received land, descendants, and greatness; but the promise of the Redeemer ran through Isaac. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic reader to distinguish the LORD's general goodness to all peoples from His particular covenant purpose — and to see that being a child of Abraham according to the flesh (as Ishmael was) is not the same as being a child of the promise (as Isaac was), a distinction the New Testament will draw out fully (Rom 9; Gal 4). The non-chosen are named, blessed, and honored; then the line of promise continues.

Now the sons of Keturah, Abraham's concubine: she bare Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah. And the sons of Jokshan; Sheba, and Dedan. And the sons of Midian; Ephah, and Epher, and Henoch, and Abida, and Eldaah. All these are the sons of Keturah. Guidance: The Chronicler records the sons of Keturah, the wife Abraham took after Sarah's death (here called his 'concubine,' marking her secondary status relative to Sarah the mother of the covenant son). From her came Midian and the other peoples of the eastern deserts and Arabian trade routes. Again the pattern holds: the wider family of Abraham is documented before the genealogy returns to the line of promise. The chapter is showing us the fruitfulness of Abraham beyond the covenant line — the LORD's word that Abraham would be 'a father of many nations' (Gen 17:4-5) was fulfilled not only through Isaac and Ishmael but through Keturah's sons, who became numerous peoples. The chapter is teaching us once more the breadth of God's providential blessing alongside the particularity of His covenant: Abraham was father to many nations, yet the promise of the Seed in whom all nations would be blessed ran through Isaac alone. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is generous in His providence to all the branches of the human family, while He pursues His redemptive purpose through the one chosen line. For the returned exiles, surrounded by these very Arabian and desert peoples descended from Abraham, the genealogy affirmed both their kinship with the nations and their distinct calling as the covenant people through Isaac. Having honored Keturah's line, the Chronicler now turns, at last, to follow Isaac — and within Isaac, the chosen son.

And Abraham begat Isaac. The sons of Isaac; Esau and Israel. The sons of Esau; Eliphaz, Reuel, and Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah. The sons of Eliphaz; Teman, and Omar, Zephi, and Gatam, Kenaz, and Timna, and Amalek. The sons of Reuel; Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. Guidance: 'Abraham begat Isaac. The sons of Isaac; Esau and Israel.' Note the Chronicler's deliberate choice of names: not 'Esau and Jacob' but 'Esau and Israel' — Jacob is named by his covenant name, the name the LORD gave him after his wrestling, the name of the nation. And again the pattern: the non-chosen brother, Esau, is dealt with first. Esau's descendants are listed — the Edomites, including Amalek (who would become Israel's bitter perennial enemy) and the clans of Teman and the rest. The chapter is showing us the principle of election sharpened to its clearest point in the two sons of Isaac. Esau was the firstborn, yet the covenant ran through the younger, Jacob/Israel — 'Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated' (Mal 1:2-3; Rom 9:13), the LORD's sovereign choice not based on birth-order or human merit. The chapter is teaching us that God's redemptive purpose is governed by His free election, not by natural precedence: the elder served the younger, and the line of promise bypassed the firstborn for the one the LORD chose. The chapter is teaching us that descent from Isaac was not enough — Esau too was Isaac's son, yet the covenant did not run through him. The naming of Jacob as 'Israel' even here in the genealogy reminds us that the chosen line is defined by the LORD's covenant naming, not merely by blood. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community, who knew Edom as a hostile neighbor that had gloated over Jerusalem's fall (Obadiah; Ps 137:7), that the ancient division between the chosen and the rejected line ran all the way back to Isaac's two sons — and that they themselves were 'Israel,' the people of the covenant name.

And Abraham begat Isaac. The sons of Isaac; Esau and Israel. The sons of Esau; Eliphaz, Reuel, and Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah. The sons of Eliphaz; Teman, and Omar, Zephi, and Gatam, Kenaz, and Timna, and Amalek. The sons of Reuel; Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. Guidance: 'Abraham begat Isaac. And the sons of Seir; Lotan, and Shobal, and Zibeon, and Anah, and Dishon, and Ezer, and Dishan. And the sons of Lotan; Hori, and Homam: and Timna was Lotan's sister. The sons of Shobal; Alian, and Manahath, and Ebal, Shephi, and Onam. And the sons of Zibeon; Aiah, and Anah. The sons of Anah; Dishon. And the sons of Dishon; Amram, and Eshban, and Ithran, and Cheran. The sons of Ezer; Bilhan, and Zavan, and Jakan. The sons of Dishan; Uz, and Aran. Guidance: The Chronicler records the sons of Seir the Horite — the original inhabitants of the land of Edom, with whom Esau's line intermarried and whom they eventually absorbed. These are not even descendants of Abraham but the indigenous clans of the region that became Edom. The Chronicler includes them to complete the picture of the Edomite peoples among whom Esau's line settled. The chapter is showing us the Chronicler's thoroughness in documenting the surrounding peoples before he sets them all aside to focus on Israel. Even the Horites, the pre-Edomite inhabitants of Seir, have their place recorded. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's knowledge and oversight extend even to the obscure clans of the nations — He is the God who numbers and names not only His covenant people but the peoples of the whole earth. The chapter is teaching us, by the sheer comprehensiveness of these lists, that nothing in human history escapes the LORD's record; the Creator who began with Adam holds every family of the earth in His view. For the returned exiles, this thoroughness underscored a comforting truth: the God who kept such careful account of all peoples certainly had not lost track of His own covenant people through the chaos of exile. Having now documented all the collateral and surrounding lines — Japheth, Ham, the wider Shem, Ishmael, Keturah, Esau, and the Horites — the Chronicler completes the Edomite record with its kings, and will then, in chapter 2, turn his full attention to Israel and the royal line of Judah.

Now these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel; Bela the son of Beor: and the name of his city was Dinhabah. And when Bela was dead, Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigned in his stead. And when Jobab was dead, Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned in his stead. And when Husham was dead, Hadad the son of Bedad, which smote Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Avith. And when Hadad was dead, Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his stead. And when Samlah was dead, Shaul of Rehoboth by the river reigned in his stead. And when Shaul was dead, Baalhanan the son of Achbor reigned in his stead. And when Baalhanan was dead, Hadad reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Pai; and his wife's name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab. Guidance: The Chronicler lists the kings of Edom 'before any king reigned over the children of Israel.' This is a pointed note. Edom had a succession of kings long before Israel ever had one — yet there is a telling difference: the Edomite kingship was not dynastic. Each king is succeeded not by his son but by an unrelated man from a different city ('when X was dead, Y of such-a-place reigned in his stead'); the crown passed from man to man without an enduring house. The chapter is showing us, by quiet contrast, what makes Israel's kingship different. Edom had kings earlier and in abundance, but its monarchy was a series of disconnected strongmen with no covenant, no promise, no enduring dynasty. Israel's kingship, when it came, would be founded on the LORD's covenant with David — an everlasting throne, a house that would not pass to strangers but would endure to the Messiah. The chapter is teaching us that earthly priority and abundance are not the same as covenant blessing: Edom got its kings first and got more of them, but they came and went like the seasons, while the LORD was preparing for Israel a King and a dynasty grounded in His own promise. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic reader, who had no king at all and might envy the seemingly more stable nations, that the absence of a present Davidic king on the throne did not mean the end of the Davidic covenant — for the difference between Edom's kingship and David's was never numbers or antiquity but the unbreakable promise of God. The whole long genealogy of chapter 1, sweeping through all the nations and even their kings, has been clearing the stage; now the Chronicler is ready to bring on the line that truly matters — the line of Israel, and within it, the royal house of Judah and David.

Edom had kings earlier and in abundance, but its monarchy was a series of disconnected strongmen with no covenant, no promise, no enduring dynasty. Israel's kingship, when it came, would be founded on the LORD's covenant with David — an everlasting throne, a house that would not pass to strangers but would endure to the Messiah. The chapter is teaching us that earthly priority and abundance are not the same as covenant blessing: Edom got its kings first and got more of them, but they came and went like the seasons, while the LORD was preparing for Israel a King and a dynasty grounded in His own promise. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic reader, who had no king at all and might envy the seemingly more stable nations, that the absence of a present Davidic king on the throne did not mean the end of the Davidic covenant — for the difference between Edom's kingship and David's was never numbers or antiquity but the unbreakable promise of God. The whole long genealogy of chapter 1, sweeping through all the nations and even their kings, has been clearing the stage; now the Chronicler is ready to bring on the line that truly matters — the line of Israel, and within it, the royal house of Judah and David.

Now these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel; Bela the son of Beor: and the name of his city was Dinhabah. And when Bela was dead, Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigned in his stead. And when Jobab was dead, Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned in his stead. And when Husham was dead, Hadad the son of Bedad, which smote Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Avith. And when Hadad was dead, Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his stead. And when Samlah was dead, Shaul of Rehoboth by the river reigned in his stead. And when Shaul was dead, Baalhanan the son of Achbor reigned in his stead. And when Baalhanan was dead, Hadad reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Pai; and his wife's name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab. Hadad died also. And the dukes of Edom were; duke Timnah, duke Aliah, duke Jetheth, Duke Aholibamah, duke Elah, duke Pinon, Duke Kenaz, duke Teman, duke Mibzar, Duke Magdiel, duke Iram. These are the dukes of Edom. Guidance: The Edomite record closes with a list of the 'dukes' (clan-chiefs, Hebrew alluphim) of Edom — Timnah, Aholibamah, Teman, and the rest. With 'Hadad died also,' the line of Edomite kings ends, and the chapter concludes the entire genealogical survey of the nations. The Chronicler has now completed his vast opening sweep: from Adam through Noah, through all the peoples descended from Japheth, Ham, and Shem, through Ishmael and Keturah's sons, through Esau's Edomites and the Horites, down to Edom's kings and chiefs. The chapter is showing us the deliberate completion of the universal frame before the narrowing to Israel. Every collateral line has been honored and recorded, and now, with the last duke of Edom named, the stage is fully set. The chapter is teaching us the grand design of the Chronicler's genealogy: it begins with all humanity (Adam) and all nations, and from that universal canvas it will now focus, in the chapters to come, on Israel — and within Israel, supremely on Judah, David, and the temple worship. This movement from the universal to the particular mirrors the whole shape of redemptive history: the LORD who is God of all the earth chose one line through which to bring blessing to all the earth. The chapter is teaching the returned exiles that they stand at the focal point of a purpose that embraces the whole world — chosen not for privilege alone but as the channel through whom the Creator of Adam and all nations would bring His salvation. With the nations all set in their place, the Chronicler now turns, in chapter 2, to 'the sons of Israel' — and the long-awaited line of Judah that leads to David and, beyond David, to David's greater Son.
